title: Track cost per winner, not creative volume — AI is about to make volume irrelevant
insight: Nathan Hudson argues that the industry's 2024 obsession with creative volume and 2025's shift to creative diversity are both about to be eclipsed by AI tools like Sora 2 and VO3. When anyone can generate unlimited high-quality video ads via automated N8N agent workflows, the bottleneck shifts entirely to ideation and creative strategy. He tracks 'cost per winner' — a metric combining production costs and ad spend testing costs — which he expects to dramatically shift downward with new AI models. The real competitive advantage becomes the ability to generate novel concepts, not produce volume.
raw_excerpt: 2024 was all about creative volume. 2025 with Meta's Andromeda has been all about creative diversity. But I think we're about to see another big shift with these new models. Neither of those things are going to be a problem. The challenge is going to be that we have so many creatives and not enough ad spend to actually test them all. We are now limited solely by our ability to come up with killer ideas. It's all about ideation and creative strategy.
source_title: How to scale Meta with dozens of AI agents — App Growth Annual 2025
actionable_steps: Start tracking 'cost per winner' as a core metric: (production cost + ad spend to validate) / number of winning creatives per period
Build an N8N or similar agent workflow that takes a creative hypothesis, generates a brief using user psychology and jobs-to-be-done, then produces video via Sora 2 or VO3
Shift creative team focus from production execution to concept ideation and creative strategy — production becomes automated
Test AI-generated UGC-style creatives that are intentionally 'obviously AI' — they perform well natively on Instagram and TikTok right now as a trending format
Use competitor ad library monitoring + LLM interpretation to generate novel concept hypotheses rather than copying competitor executions

title: Copying competitor ads with AI will lead to concept fatigue — the winners will be original concept generators
insight: Nathan Hudson warns that while it's now trivially easy to clone competitor ads from the Facebook Ad Library using AI tools, this approach will lead to massive concept fatigue and trend fatigue at scale. When everyone has access to the same AI creative tools and the same competitor inspiration, the differentiator becomes original concept generation. The competitive moat shifts from creative production capabilities to creative strategy and ideation quality.
raw_excerpt: It's never been so easy to just copy competitors. You can literally see what ads they're running on Facebook ad library, clone them with AI, and push them live. But I don't think that's going to work. I think that's going to lead to crazy concept fatigue and trend fatigue. And the winners are going to be those people who are finding new concepts and are really focusing on creative strategy.
source_title: How to scale Meta with dozens of AI agents — App Growth Annual 2025
actionable_steps: Use competitor ad library as inspiration input, not as a source to copy — feed competitor concepts into an LLM to generate differentiated variations, not clones
Invest in user research (reviews, interviews, in-app analytics) to source original concept ideas that competitors haven't discovered
Build a concept tracking system: log every concept tested, its origin (competitor-inspired vs. original), and performance — track whether original concepts outperform copies over time
Allocate at least 30% of creative testing budget to radical/novel concepts that don't exist in any competitor's ad library

title: Mini landing pages work as a creative testing hack — same results as app campaigns but with reliable ad-level data
insight: Jessica Vicho explains that running Meta website sales campaigns with mini landing pages (instead of app promotion campaigns) can serve as a creative testing channel with reliable, ad-level attribution data. In her tests, the same winning creatives won on both app campaigns and mini landing pages, but the mini landing pages provided immediate, granular data. The audience reached tends to be slightly older with more spending power. Attribution is triggered on-page via JavaScript that collects UTM parameters and fires to the MMP, which then auto-redirects to the app store.
raw_excerpt: I like to use mini landing pages for creative testing because what I notice is that although the CPMs are high, the results are usually the same and the CTRs are also higher. So you end up having a similar cost per trial. And it gives you data right away — reliable data at the ad level. I did a test where I was trying to do creative testing on app and mini landing pages and the results were the same. The same creative won.
source_title: App Growth Annual Lightning Workshops — Web to App
actionable_steps: Set up a simple mini landing page (copy + image + CTA button) with on-page JavaScript that fires attribution to your MMP (Adjust, Singular, or AppsFlyer) before redirecting to the app store
Launch website sales campaigns on Meta (not app promotion) optimized for a web event, targeting mobile users — check campaign settings carefully as mobile-only targeting is hidden
Use the MMP partner tracker for 'Facebook Web' to connect in-app events back as web events — on Adjust and Singular you can send all events, not just attributed ones
Run creative tests on mini landing pages in parallel with app campaigns to validate that winners correlate — then use mini landing pages for faster iteration with better data
If you lack resources for a proper landing page, use your MMP's auto-redirect feature as a lighter alternative, but expect some data loss on attribution parameters

title: Web-to-web onboarding funnels work best for products that need explanation — plan for 50% failed payment rates
insight: Jessica Vicho outlines when web-to-web funnels (40+ screen onboarding flows ending in web purchase) make sense: products that are hard to explain, new verticals people don't know about, and products that benefit from emotional triggers during onboarding. The purchase happens on web, bypassing the App Store commission. Key operational realities: expect ~50% failed payment rates on web, aim for profitability after first renewal (not immediately), and go for direct purchase with shorter plans (monthly/quarterly/weekly) rather than annual-only. Don't copy competitor onboarding flows — tailor them to your product.
raw_excerpt: Web onboarding works especially good with channels that are not intentional — discovery channels like TikTok or Meta. It makes sense when you need more explanation, your product is a new vertical, you can leverage emotional triggers during onboarding, and you can offer immediate value. Remember there's a high failed payment rate around 50%. My recommendation: go for direct purchase, make sure you have shorter plans. Look at being profitable not right away but after the first renewal because your renewal on web are going to be great.
source_title: App Growth Annual Lightning Workshops — Web to App
actionable_steps: Evaluate if your product fits web-to-web: does it need explanation? Is it a new category? Can you create emotional engagement during onboarding? If no to all three, stick with app funnels
Structure your web onboarding in template blocks: landing, question, upselling, cheering — then organize into sections and focus on creating aha moments in the first few screens
Target 60-80% progression to screen 2, 20% reaching the paywall, and 1-5% purchase — if drop-off is on screen 2, fix that before optimizing the paywall
Offer shorter billing plans (monthly, quarterly, even weekly) for web purchases — model profitability including the ~50% failed payment rate, targeting break-even after first renewal
Align your ad creatives with the onboarding flow content — creatives pre-qualify the audience, so misalignment between ad messaging and onboarding screens kills conversion

title: Focus on emotional jobs-to-be-done over functional ones across your full funnel — painkillers beat vitamins
insight: Daphne Tideman advocates for structuring your entire funnel (ads, onboarding, paywalls, retention) around emotional jobs-to-be-done rather than functional product descriptions. Apps that position as 'painkillers' (solving an unmet need people will pay for) vastly outperform 'vitamins' (nice-to-have). The key distinction: don't say 'smart GPS tracker for dogs' — say 'never lose sight of your escape artist, even off leash.' Emotional drivers like feeling secure, feeling in control, or standing out from the crowd are stronger conversion triggers than feature lists. Jobs-to-be-done evolve over time, so periodically re-interview users to catch shifts.
raw_excerpt: Too many apps talk about the features but don't talk about how those features help people achieve their jobs to be done. They talk about the category and what the product does instead of what that customer is trying to achieve. Focus on what they want to feel and the change they want to see rather than just the functional job. Rather than 'we help you organize your day and your tasks' — no, you're helping them feel in control and capable instead of feeling overwhelmed. Emotional jobs are stronger than functional jobs.
source_title: App Growth Annual Lightning Workshops — Jobs to Be Done
actionable_steps: Run a jobs-to-be-done audit: review-mine your app store reviews and competitor reviews to identify the top 3-5 emotional pain points users describe in their own language
Rewrite your paywall CTA to reflect relief or transformation (the emotional job) rather than features — test against your current feature-list paywall
Survey renewed subscribers (not just new users) to identify if jobs-to-be-done have evolved — compare newer users vs. long-term users for diverging needs
Redesign at least one ad creative to lead with the emotional pain point rather than the product category — e.g., 'never lose your dog again' vs. 'GPS pet tracker'
Map the 10 emotional drivers (feeling secure, in control, standing out, etc.) to your product and test messaging variants for each in ads and onboarding

title: Hybrid monetization should start with upselling power users, not converting free users — the high ceiling is easier than the low floor
insight: Thomas Petit recommends starting hybrid monetization from the 'high ceiling' side: finding ways to extract more value from your most engaged paying subscribers, rather than trying to monetize the 90% of free users who don't pay. Power users who love your product are willing to pay for premium tiers, coaching, exclusive features, or adjacent products. Games demonstrate this — within payers, a handful generate half the revenue. The approach: interview your power users about what they'd want beyond the current subscription, and let the opportunity surface naturally rather than picking tactics (ads, AP, e-com) from a list.
raw_excerpt: I actually started on the other side which is once you have paid subscribers who retain and are really power users, they love your product, they actually would be willing to do more with you. I think that's easier than fighting against what doesn't work. Once you have a bit of product market fit and some users that love your product, it's probably easier to find upsells and other models for that power user crowd than it is to convince the 90% to do anything with your app.
source_title: Why Subscription Apps Are Adding Usage-Based Pricing in 2026 — Sub Club Live
actionable_steps: Identify your top 10% of subscribers by engagement and spending — survey them about what additional features, content, or services they'd pay for
Introduce a premium tier at 2-3x your current subscription price with power-user features (personalized coaching, AI-powered features, advanced analytics, community access)
For apps with AI costs per usage, implement usage-based pricing or higher tiers to ensure power users cover their actual compute costs
Test affiliate partnerships for adjacent products your power users already buy (e.g., fishing equipment for a fishing app, gym gear for a fitness app) before building e-commerce yourself
Don't attempt hybrid monetization as a substitute for product-market fit — if nobody's paying for your subscription, the problem is product, not pricing model

title: AI features create variable costs that break the traditional subscription model — tiered or usage-based pricing is now a necessity
insight: Thomas Petit explains that AI-powered features fundamentally change subscription app economics because they introduce meaningful per-user variable costs (API calls, model inference). The traditional flat subscription model where serving 1 user vs. 1 million costs the same no longer holds. This forces apps to rethink pricing: either implement usage caps per tier, introduce higher-priced tiers for heavy usage, or move to consumption-based pricing. Some apps discovered their power users were actually unprofitable because their AI usage exceeded the subscription price.
raw_excerpt: Most subscriptions for all this time were basically zero cost product. Whether you serve a million users or one is virtually the same cost. And if you have any AI based feature, this changes a lot because the more users use the app, the more it incurs cost. I used to work with an app whose core feature is AI based. They realized that those power users were not profitable at all because even though they were paying $10 a month, they were generating even more cost.
source_title: Why Subscription Apps Are Adding Usage-Based Pricing in 2026 — Sub Club Live
actionable_steps: Calculate your per-user AI cost: total AI API/inference spend divided by active users per tier — identify if any user segments are unprofitable
Implement usage caps or throttling on AI features for lower tiers, with higher limits for premium tiers
Model a consumption-based pricing layer (e.g., credits or tokens for AI features) on top of your base subscription — test willingness to pay with your highest-usage cohort first
If introducing a higher tier, frame it around value (more AI generations, faster processing, premium models) rather than punishing usage on the base tier

title: Measure creative success by cost per successful creative, not by total volume tested — prioritize hypothesis quality over quantity
insight: Alper Taner argues against the industry trend of bragging about testing 500+ creatives per month. What matters is cost per successful creative (one that beats BAU performance), not raw volume. High volume often leads to shallow analysis, rushed hypothesis formation, and disconnect between the person running campaigns and the person briefing creatives. He recommends investing more effort in the planning and hypothesis phase — success of a test is at least 50% dependent on how you plan, strategize, and hypothesize, not on execution alone.
raw_excerpt: What matters at the end of the day is your success rate and not the amount of creatives. LinkedIn is full of 'we tested 500 creatives, we tested 700.' Those numbers alone don't matter if your success rate is very low. I would more look at your cost per successful creative that is beating the BAU ads performance. I believe the success of a test is at least 50% dependent on how you plan it, how you strategize it, how you hypothesize it. Execution is just the what part.
source_title: Creative Testing, Data-Driven Decisions, and Growing Mobile Apps — Sub Club Podcast
actionable_steps: Define and track 'cost per successful creative': (total production cost + total ad spend on testing) / number of creatives that beat BAU performance
Before producing any creative, document the hypothesis: what variable are you testing, what's the expected outcome, and what data informed the hypothesis
Merge three data inputs for hypothesis formation: (1) first-party ad performance data, (2) in-app analytics on high-LTV user behavior, (3) third-party competitor and trend data
When a concept appears to win, validate in phases: test the specific variable (is it the dog? or any animal? or something cute?), then iterate — never jump to 200 variants of the first signal

title: Don't let Meta's algorithm pick creative winners too early — democratize spend across ads in test ad sets
insight: Alper Taner warns that Meta's algorithm often makes premature decisions in the first 5,000-10,000 impressions, giving 90% of spend to one creative while starving others that could have performed equally well or better. He's observed repeatedly that when the dominant ad is paused, the other ads 'bloom' and perform well. His approach: use cumulative spend and cumulative performance curves to determine your minimum viable test threshold, then manually ensure all creatives in a test ad set reach that threshold before making winner/loser calls.
raw_excerpt: Just because algorithm decides to deliver one creative over others doesn't make the other creatives bad. That particular creative that got the 90% of the spend was maybe lucky. We call it 'lucky 5,000 impressions.' I've seen that multiple times where you take out that dominant ad from that adset and all the other ads suddenly bloom. I personally try to get spend on the other creatives if I see a dominant one. I believe that every creative should have an equal chance.
source_title: Creative Testing, Data-Driven Decisions, and Growing Mobile Apps — Sub Club Podcast
actionable_steps: Plot cumulative spend vs. cumulative cost-per-action for each creative to find your minimum decision threshold — the point where metrics stabilize (often far below what most accounts spend before killing a creative)
When one creative captures 80-90% of ad set spend, pause it and observe if other creatives recover — don't label under-delivering creatives as losers based on algorithmic allocation alone
Separate creative testing ad sets from BAU ad sets: in testing, enforce minimum spend thresholds per creative; in BAU, let the algorithm optimize freely
When a creative hits your minimum decision threshold and proves to be a winner in the test ad set, move it to a BAU ad set rather than letting it cannibalize other tests
Tier your geo targeting by CAC or LTV to prevent large markets (e.g., US) from dominating spend and starving smaller markets of data

title: Map your trial event honestly to Meta — sending trial-as-purchase inflates your auction costs and sends mixed signals
insight: Alper Taner shares a case where simply remapping the trial event from 'purchase' to 'trial' (the honest event) in Meta Events Manager reduced cost per trial by ~35%. The reasoning: when subscription apps map trial-start to purchase, they compete in the same auction as e-commerce advertisers willing to pay $200 per purchase. The algorithm then serves ads to general purchasers rather than trial-likely users. Sending a clear, logical event funnel helps Meta's algorithm understand your business model and find the right users. Mixed or illogical event mappings (e.g., cancellation mapped to add-to-cart) confuse the algorithm's eCPM calculations.
raw_excerpt: All we did was mapping their trial event to trial and their cost per purchase/actual cost per trial went down by around 35%. Only with that change. Same creatives, same campaigns, just different event optimization. If everyone is optimizing towards purchase, the guy whose AOV is $200 is willing to pay up to $200. How are you going to compete with that? When you map your trial to trial, you're honest about your intentions. The only way for the algorithm to know your business are the events you send.
source_title: Creative Testing, Data-Driven Decisions, and Growing Mobile Apps — Sub Club Podcast
actionable_steps: Audit your Meta Events Manager event mapping: check if your trial-start is mapped to purchase, subscribe, or trial — verify the funnel logic makes sense from Meta's perspective
If cost per trial is high and you're mapping trial to purchase, create a duplicate trial event in your MMP, map it to 'subscribe' or 'trial', and run an A/B split test for 2+ weeks
Keep event funnels logical and clean: don't send every possible event to Meta — only send events that form a coherent conversion funnel the algorithm can learn from
Remove any event mappings that don't make funnel sense (e.g., cancellation mapped to add-to-cart, subscription mapped to a non-standard event)
Don't change event mapping if your current setup is working and profitable — only test alternatives when you have a clear hypothesis for why the current mapping is suboptimal

title: Use the cap-inflated budget strategy on Meta to bypass pacing throttling at scale — but only with strict guardrails
insight: Alper Taner describes a Meta-recommended technique for large advertisers: set your campaign daily budget to an artificially high number (e.g., $1M/day) while controlling actual spend through campaign spend limits and bid caps. This prevents Meta's pacing algorithm from throttling morning delivery to conserve budget for evening peaks. The result: more aggressive delivery throughout the day, capturing higher-quality traffic at stable performance. Critical guardrails: always set campaign spend limits, use bid caps (start 10-20% below target CPA), and this is specifically for mature accounts already spending $50K+/day.
raw_excerpt: We tested this to bypass the pacing function. We put a million dollars a day budget at the campaign level with campaign spend limits in place. It did work quite well because we were able to capture a lot more higher quality traffic. The way the daily budget works, it divides the budget to 24 hours. By putting a million a day with budget caps and spending limits at every level, you bypass the auction dynamics. Start conservatively — if your cost per purchase is 50, start around 45. This is not for beginners. This works well at large accounts with large learnings that are spending 50k a day anyway.
source_title: Creative Testing, Data-Driven Decisions, and Growing Mobile Apps — Sub Club Podcast
actionable_steps: Only attempt this at scale ($50K+/day accounts): set campaign daily budget to $1M while setting a campaign spend limit at your actual desired daily spend
Use bid caps as your primary cost control (start 10-20% below your target CPA) — the inflated budget prevents pacing throttling while bid caps control unit economics
Scale incrementally: start with a campaign spend limit at your current daily spend, validate performance, then raise the limit in $10-20K increments
Monitor delivery pacing hourly for the first few days — you should see more even distribution throughout the day vs. concentrated evening delivery
Never use this technique without campaign spend limits — the inflated budget is designed to bypass pacing, so without guardrails you will massively overspend
